[𝐍𝐨𝐫𝐦𝐚𝐥 𝐓𝐞𝐜𝐡 𝐒𝐞𝐫𝐢𝐞𝐬 #4] Why are we even discussing AGI?? Every major tech leap started with a real problem to solve; something broken, inefficient, or missing. So here’s my question: what problem is AGI trying to solve? What’s the “why” behind all this? Because honestly, a lot of what’s happening right now feels like art for art’s sake. Interesting discussions, yes, but directionless. In [Series #1], I mentioned that what we call “AI misalignment” is often just human behavior scaled, persuasion, exaggeration, selective truth. Models simply reflect what we reward, and since our systems reward attention over accuracy, the models optimize for that too. But maybe the bigger issue isn’t just how we train models. Maybe it’s how we represent knowledge in the first place. We keep throwing more text at models as if language is the ultimate format for intelligence. It’s not. If I need 300 words to explain something that a simple diagram could make clear in 5 seconds, then clearly text isn’t the best representation. And even diagrams aren’t enough sometimes. So maybe it’s time to rethink data representation, not just model scaling. Current LLMs follow the “Big Data Mindset”. They learn by sampling billions of disconnected text and scaling that up with huge compute. It works to an extent, but it also creates deep blind spots: * No Experiential Learning: These models don’t learn from continuous experiences like humans do. They don’t build causality over time, or form memories that evolve with context. Humans do, that’s how we reason across situations, adapt, and form nuance. * No Continual Learning: Stop feeding a model internet-scale data and it stops learning. Meanwhile, a baby with almost no memory can generalize from a few examples without millions of gradient descent steps. LLMs have great memory, sure, but they don’t generalize the way we do. So maybe the path forward isn’t “bigger data” or “smarter models”. Maybe we should ask simpler questions: * What problem are we actually solving with AGI? * How can we represent knowledge in a form that goes beyond text? (The multimodality discussion) * And how can we make models that learn like humans, continuously, contextually, from experience, not just from data dumps? Maybe the next leap in AI won’t come from more data or bigger models, but from rethinking how knowledge itself is represented. More soon.

The AGI Mirage: Why LLMs Are Burning Time and Fueling a Defense Bubble The recent dust-up over A Definition of AGI by Dan Hendrycks should be a wake-up call for anyone betting on AI to transform national security. The paper claimed GPT-5 had achieved “58% AGI.” That headline collapsed almost overnight when Michael Saxon exposed two fabricated citations on October 18, 2025. The references looked real. They weren’t. And no one caught it before publication. This wasn’t a fluke. It was a preview of how hype gets ahead of verification. LLMs are built to sound authoritative, not to deliver truth. A phrase like “60% invasion likelihood” isn’t an insight — it’s a probabilistic guess wrapped in confident language. And the United States Department of Defense (DOD) has poured hundreds of millions of dollars (e.g., a $200M partnership with OpenAI) into operationalizing exactly this kind of output. Here’s the dirty secret: every LLM output must be validated before it can influence real-world decisions. The CDAO RAI Toolkit and DIU Guidelines require analysts to check each assessment against ground-truth sources like SIGINT and satellite imagery using SHAP, ART, and T&E Toolkit. Yes, an LLM can spit out an answer in seconds. But it’s worth nothing until traditional analytic tools corroborate it. If it can’t be confirmed, it’s simply rejected. In practice, decision-making still moves at the speed of traditional methods — not the AI. The LLM may get there first, but its answer isn’t usable until the old systems agree. This is where the bubble shows. Programs are funding LLMs not because they accelerate decisions, but because they look like they should. They generate slick numbers and impressive dashboards. But they don’t change the operational clock. They add complexity, demand human-in-the-loop checks, and increase the risk of fabricated “facts” like Hendrycks’ citations. Even when validated, LLMs struggle with structured, high-dimensional numerical data. Unlike specialized models, they aren’t built for precision. Their strength is fast synthesis, not verified truth. That makes them useful for aggregation — not for driving real-time operational calls. The result is a costly paradox: 1. LLMs produce fast but probabilistic answers. 2. Those answers must be verified by traditional tools. 3. Unverified outputs are discarded. 4. Decision speed remains tied to the old methods. The promise of LLMs in defense isn’t inherently wrong — but pretending they’re decision accelerators is how bubbles form: overpromise, underdeliver, overspend. Until the validation bottleneck is solved, LLMs will remain expensive confidence boosters, not force multipliers. The Hendrycks episode wasn’t an outlier — it was a warning. In national security, speed without trust is useless, and trust still lives with the traditional tools.

# Altman shares vision of AI's future
-
"Deep learning worked," OpenAI chief Sam Altman declared in a new blog post titled "The Intelligence Age." Altman claimed that "in the next couple of decades, we will be able to do things that would have seemed like magic to our grandparents." In an extremely upbeat assessment of the future of artificial intelligence, he also wrote that we might have "superintelligence" in a few thousand days, and touted AI's potential to improve humanity's progress in areas including healthcare, education and overall prosperity. Speaking of prosperity: OpenAI is eyeing a $150 billion valuation, per The New York Times.

On March 16, 2021, two years before the launch of GPT-4 (March, 13, 2023), Sam Altman published Moore’s Law for Everything. The post went largely unnoticed by business and government leaders, but 20 months later the introduction of ChatGPT would wake the world up to what was happening at the leading AI research labs. In the post Altman wrote: “My work at OpenAI reminds me every day about the magnitude of the socioeconomic change that is coming sooner than most people believe. . . . The coming change will center around the most impressive of our capabilities: the phenomenal ability to think, create, understand, and reason. To the three great technological revolutions–the agricultural, the industrial, and the computational–we will add a fourth: the AI revolution. This revolution will generate enough wealth for everyone to have what they need, if we as a society manage it responsibly.” Now, Altman is back with another prophetic post, “The Intelligence Age.” In the article, Altman writes: “It won’t happen all at once, but we’ll soon be able to work with AI that helps us accomplish much more than we ever could without AI; eventually we can each have a personal AI team, full of virtual experts in different areas, working together to create almost anything we can imagine. Our children will have virtual tutors who can provide personalized instruction in any subject, in any language, and at whatever pace they need. We can imagine similar ideas for better healthcare, the ability to create any kind of software someone can imagine, and much more. With these new abilities, we can have shared prosperity to a degree that seems unimaginable today.” He goes on to say that not only will we reach AGI (artificial general intelligence) soon, but ASI (artificial superintelligence), when AI is smarter than the smartest humans at all cognitive tasks: “It is possible that we will have superintelligence in a few thousand days (!); it may take longer, but I’m confident we’ll get there.” How did we arrive at this moment of an intelligence explosion? He continues: “In 15 words: deep learning worked, got predictably better with scale, and we dedicated increasing resources to it. “That’s really it; humanity discovered an algorithm that could really, truly learn any distribution of data (or really, the underlying “rules” that produce any distribution of data). To a shocking degree of precision, the more compute and data available, the better it gets at helping people solve hard problems. I find that no matter how much time I spend thinking about this, I can never really internalize how consequential it is. “Technology brought us from the Stone Age to the Agricultural Age and then to the Industrial Age. From here, the path to the Intelligence Age is paved with compute, energy, and human will.” I hope our business and government leaders are listening this time. https://ia.samaltman.com/
